skill.name: self-improvement
skill.description: Captures AI assistant learnings, command errors, user corrections, and reusable workflow patterns in local markdown files for later review.
skill.summary: Record corrections, errors, and reusable agent lessons in local learning files.
skill.author: peterskoett

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, and weak-crypto patterns, but most are markdown examples, inline code formatting, or safety warnings rather than executable threats. The confirmed risks are opt-in hook scripts, hidden agent configuration paths, environment-variable inspection of command output, and local file scaffolding; I found no evidence of malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or prompt-injection instructions aimed at overriding the evaluator.

medium_findings.title: Opt-In Agent Hooks Execute Local Scripts
medium_findings.description: The skill documents hook configurations that run local shell scripts on prompt submission and after Bash tool use. The reviewed scripts print reminders and do not exfiltrate data, but installed hooks run with the agent user permissions and can inspect command output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hook configuration and scripts are directly present, and the behavior is clear. Confidence is below very high because the scripts are opt-in and currently only emit reminder text.

medium_findings.title: Hidden Agent Configuration and Workspace Paths
medium_findings.description: The setup guides instruct users to create hidden learning directories and install hooks under OpenClaw or Claude configuration paths. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it creates persistent agent behavior that should be reviewed before enabling globally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly reference hidden directories and hook installation paths. The risk depends on user installation choices, so the confidence is high but not absolute.

low_findings.title: Command and Backtick Static Hits Are Mostly Documentation
low_findings.description: Most external-command findings are markdown code fences, inline command examples, or templates for users to run manually. I did not find evidence that the skill automatically executes those documented commands without opt-in hook setup.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged patterns are visibly markdown examples or inline formatting in the reviewed files. A user can still copy commands manually, but that is not automatic command execution by the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and Private-Key Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography findings appear to come from ordinary words in markdown such as description or status text, and the private-key finding is a warning not to log private keys. No cryptographic operation or secret material was found in the reviewed lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is documentation and redaction guidance, not crypto implementation. Confidence is high because no code path performs hashing, encryption, or key handling there.

low_findings.title: Network References Are Installation and Attribution Links
low_findings.description: Network findings are GitHub and specification URLs in documentation. They do not create automatic outbound requests, although the documented git clone command would contact GitHub if a user runs it manually.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are static documentation links and install examples. Confidence is high because I found no fetch, curl, or network call in executable code.

low_findings.title: Local Skill Scaffold Script Has Bounded Writes
low_findings.description: The extraction helper creates a skill directory and SKILL.md from a user-provided name, but it validates the name and rejects absolute output paths and parent-directory traversal. This reduces the filesystem risk to expected local scaffolding behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The validation and write locations are explicit in the script. Confidence is high for bounded local writes, while residual risk remains because it still writes files when run.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hook Command Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation shows agent hook configuration that runs local scripts during prompt and tool events. This pattern is powerful and should remain opt-in with clear review of script contents.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hook command pattern is explicit and executable when users install it. The reviewed script contents are limited, so this is a controlled dangerous pattern rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Output Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: The error detector reads CLAUDE_TOOL_OUTPUT and searches for error strings. It does not print the captured output, but this pattern can expose sensitive command output if future changes log or forward it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment access and matching behavior are directly present. The current script avoids printing the sensitive value, so the risk is potential rather than active exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Hidden Directory Setup
dangerous_patterns.description: Several setup paths use hidden directories for agent configuration, OpenClaw hooks, and learning files. Hidden paths are expected for these tools but can make persistent behavior less visible to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hidden paths and persistence are directly documented. The behavior is user-directed setup, so confidence is moderate-high rather than very high for security impact.

content.user_title: Capture AI Coding Lessons Across Sessions
content.value_statement: AI assistants often repeat mistakes when lessons stay buried in chat history. This skill records corrections, errors, and reusable patterns in local learning files for future review.
actual_capabilities: Creates and uses local .learnings files for lessons, errors, and feature requests.
Defines structured formats for learning, error, and feature request entries.
Provides optional Claude Code, Codex, and OpenClaw hook setup examples.
Prints reminder text through hook scripts when enabled by the user.
Detects error-like command output through a simple environment-variable check.
Includes a helper script to scaffold new skills from valuable learnings.
limitations: It does not verify that logged lessons are correct or complete.
Hook automation is opt-in and must be configured by the user.
The error detector uses string matching and can miss or overreport errors.
Learning files can contain sensitive context if users ignore redaction guidance.

use_cases.title: Preserve Debugging Lessons
use_cases.description: Record non-obvious failures, fixes, and project gotchas after difficult debugging sessions.
use_cases.target_user: Individual developers using AI coding assistants

use_cases.title: Share Team Agent Memory
use_cases.description: Promote repeated lessons into project guidance files so future agent sessions follow known conventions.

use_cases.title: Build Reusable Skills
use_cases.description: Turn recurring solved problems into standalone skill templates with clear usage criteria.
use_cases.target_user: AI workflow maintainers and skill authors

prompt_templates.title: Review Existing Lessons
prompt_templates.prompt: Before starting this task, review .learnings/LEARNINGS.md and .learnings/ERRORS.md for relevant lessons. Summarize only items that apply.

prompt_templates.title: Log a New Correction
prompt_templates.scenario: After the user corrects an assistant mistake
prompt_templates.prompt: Log this correction in .learnings/LEARNINGS.md using the self-improvement format. Redact secrets and include the practical rule to follow next time.

prompt_templates.title: Capture a Debugging Error
prompt_templates.scenario: After resolving an unexpected command or tool failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an error entry for the issue we just fixed. Include the failed operation, root cause, resolution, and a concise prevention note.

prompt_templates.title: Promote a Recurring Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Find recurring learning entries with related causes. Recommend which should be promoted to AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, or a new reusable skill.

output_examples.input: A build failed because the wrong package manager was used, and the user explained the project requires pnpm.
output_examples.output: A learning entry records the correction, marks the area as tooling, and adds a prevention rule to use pnpm for this project.

output_examples.input: A repeated Docker platform issue appears in several learning entries.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends promoting the pattern into project guidance and creating a reusable skill if the fix applies across repositories.

output_examples.input: A command output contains a secret while debugging an API failure.
output_examples.output: The assistant logs a redacted summary, avoids storing the secret, and notes the safe reproduction steps instead of raw output.

best_practices: Log lessons immediately after corrections or non-obvious fixes while context is fresh.
Redact secrets, tokens, raw transcripts, and full command outputs by default.
Promote recurring lessons into stable project guidance only after they are proven useful.
anti_patterns: Do not use learning files as a dump for full logs or private configuration.
Do not enable global hooks before reviewing the script paths and contents.
Do not promote one-off mistakes into permanent guidance without checking recurrence.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically modify my project?
faq.answer: No. The main workflow is instructional. File creation and hook behavior occur only when the user runs the setup commands or enables hooks.

faq.question: Where are learnings stored?
faq.answer: The skill uses local .learnings markdown files for lessons, errors, and feature requests. OpenClaw examples also show workspace-level locations.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation includes manual workflows and hook examples for Claude Code, Codex, and OpenClaw-style environments.

faq.question: Should learning files be committed to Git?
faq.answer: It depends on team policy. Keep them local for private notes, or share sanitized entries when the team benefits from common guidance.

faq.question: How does the error detector work?
faq.answer: The optional hook reads command output from an environment variable and prints a reminder when common error strings appear.

faq.question: What security precautions matter most?
faq.answer: Review hook scripts before enabling them, avoid global hooks unless needed, and never store secrets or raw transcripts in learning files.
